guides.name: Content Brief
guides.tagline: Writer-ready SEO briefs from a target keyword in under 20 minutes instead of three hours.
guides.what:
Content Brief starts from a target keyword and ends with a complete, writer-ready SEO brief: search intent classification, audience and angle, a structured outline with a word-count target, internal link suggestions pulled from your own site, and a handoff prompt for Claude or Cursor. Every brief lands in the same standardized .md template, so writers stop guessing what a good brief looks like.

Briefing is the bottleneck in most content operations, which is why this compounds. A brief that took a strategist around three hours now takes under 20 minutes, so the same team covers more of the keyword map without lowering the bar. And because every brief follows the same template and logs its sources, the process is inspectable, repeatable, and survives staff turnover.
guides.outcome: Brief production time drops from roughly three hours to under 20 minutes per brief, so a keyword can go from queue to writer-ready the same day.
prereqs: A keyword research tool you already use (Semrush, Ahrefs, or similar) with export or API access
A documented content process: who requests briefs, who writes, who approves
An inventory of your site's URLs (sitemap or a Screaming Frog crawl) for internal-link suggestions
Read access to Google Search Console for your domain, to sanity-check intent against real queries

manualPath.overview: This version runs on keyword exports, a Google Sheet, and a saved prompt pack in Claude — no APIs, no middleware. It suits teams producing a handful of briefs a week who want the templates and structure proven before wiring anything together. You gather the data; the prompts do the assembly; an editor signs off.

steps.title: Set up the brief template and intent rubric
steps.body: Create a .md brief template with fixed sections: audience, angle, outline, word-count target, internal links, and a handoff prompt block. Write a one-page rubric for classifying keywords as informational, commercial, or navigational. Get your writers to approve the template before anything else — it is the contract the whole workflow fills in.

steps.title: Export your keyword queue
steps.body: Export target keywords from your keyword tool into a Google Sheet with volume and difficulty columns. Add a status column (queued, briefed, drafted, published) and an owner column. This sheet is now the single queue every brief comes from.

steps.title: Capture the SERP by hand
steps.body: For each queued keyword, run the search and record the top 10 results in a SERP analysis tab: titles, recurring H2 themes, approximate word counts, and whether a featured snippet is present. Ten minutes of structured looking beats an hour of unstructured browsing.

steps.title: Run the prompt pack in Claude
steps.body: Paste the keyword, your intent classification, and the SERP notes into a saved Claude prompt that outputs the brief in your exact template. Save the result as a .md file in a shared briefs folder. Keep the prompt wording in a dated doc so you can see which revision produced which brief.

steps.title: Add internal links from your URL inventory
steps.body: Check your sitemap sheet or Screaming Frog crawl export and pick two or three genuinely relevant internal links. Paste them into the brief's internal-links section with one line of anchor guidance each.

steps.title: Editor review and writer handoff
steps.body: An editor reads every brief before it reaches a writer — checking angle, outline, and links against the rubric. Log the date, keyword, prompt used, and approver in the tracking sheet, then flip the status to briefed and hand off with the Claude/Cursor prompt attached.

manualPath.cadence: Batch weekly against the keyword queue; expect roughly 20 minutes per brief once the template and prompt pack are settled.

mcpPath.overview: Here the classify → SERP → outline → brief loop runs as an agent workflow. Keyword data, Search Console, and your site inventory connect through read-scoped API or MCP access, and the agent works a queue instead of waiting on a strategist. It suits teams briefing at volume that already have a documented content process and a real editorial gate.

steps.title: Wire the data sources, read-only
steps.body: Connect your keyword tool and Google Search Console to the agent using read-only credentials, and point it at a sheet or export of your site's URL inventory. The agent gets no CMS login and no publishing access of any kind — it reads data and writes brief files, nothing else.

steps.title: Define the queue and the trigger
steps.body: The brief queue lives in a Google Sheet or your project tracker. A new row with status queued triggers a run — via n8n, Make, or Zapier, or on a scheduled agent check. Anyone on the team can request a brief by adding a keyword; nobody has to know how the machinery works.

steps.title: Let the agent run the brief loop
steps.body: For each queued keyword the agent classifies intent, pulls SERP and keyword data, checks Search Console for related queries you already rank for, generates the outline and word-count target, and selects internal links from your URL inventory. It writes the finished brief into your .md template in the shared briefs folder.

steps.title: Record every run
steps.body: Each run appends an audit entry: keyword, data sources pulled, prompt used, timestamp, and output file location. If a brief looks off, you can trace exactly what the agent saw and regenerate it against the same inputs.

steps.title: Hold the approval gate
steps.body: The agent posts each finished brief as pending — a Slack message or a status flip in the queue. An editor approves it or sends it back with notes; only approved briefs move to writer handoff with the Claude/Cursor prompt attached. The agent never advances a brief past the gate on its own.

connections: Your keyword research tool (read-only API access or an MCP server for it)
Google Search Console (read-only) for query and ranking data
Google Sheets or your project tracker — the brief queue and audit log
Google Drive or your docs store, where finished .md briefs land
Slack or email for pending-approval notifications

tools: Google Sheets — the keyword queue, status tracking, and SERP worksheet in the manual path
Claude — runs the prompt pack by hand, or the full agent loop via MCP servers
Semrush or Ahrefs — keyword volume, difficulty, and SERP data by export or API
Screaming Frog — crawls your site to build the internal-link inventory
Google Search Console — real query data to validate intent classification
n8n, Make, or Zapier — triggers the agent when a keyword enters the queue
guardrails: No CMS access, period: the agent reads keyword, SERP, and Search Console data and writes only brief files — no publishing scope of any kind
Every brief passes an editor approval gate before writer handoff — nothing enters the content calendar unreviewed
Per-run audit log records keyword, data sources, prompt used, and output file, so any brief can be traced and regenerated
Fully reversible: briefs are files in a pending folder — rejecting one discards a document, never published content
guides.metaDescription: How to build the Content Brief automation: a manual keyword-export and spreadsheet path, or an MCP-connected agent that drafts SEO briefs with human approval.

guides.name: Campaign Launch Kit
guides.tagline: Brief in, ready-to-launch campaign kit out — and nothing spends until you approve it.
guides.what:
Campaign Launch Kit assembles a complete, ready-to-upload campaign from a one-page brief: a strategic brief, an ad group structure organized by theme, intent tier, and match type, three copy variants per ad group, landing page outlines mapped to each group's intent, a documented UTM taxonomy, and a pre-launch QA checklist covering tracking, copy, targeting, budget, and landing page parity. Everything arrives paused — no spend starts until a human enables it.

Every launch feeds the same templates and a persistent instruction file that records your offer, audience, naming conventions, and guardrails — so kit two is faster and more consistent than kit one. Setup drops from days to hours, and clean UTMs from day one mean every downstream report can be trusted.
guides.outcome: Campaign setup time drops from days to hours, with every launch shipping a consistent structure, clean UTMs, and a passed QA checklist.
prereqs: A documented launch process — the steps and sign-offs your team already follows; the automation encodes it, it can't invent it
Consistent naming conventions for campaigns, ad groups, and UTMs
Offer, audience/ICP, and brand tone-of-voice documentation the kit generator can draw from
A project management tool where launch requests live (needed for the MCP path)
Access to your ad account — Google Ads Editor for the manual path, API access for the MCP path

manualPath.overview: You run the kit from templates: a brief document, a Google Sheets workbook, and Claude in a chat window. There are no system connections — you paste the brief in, paste the kit out, and upload through Google Ads Editor yourself. It suits teams launching a campaign or two a month who want the speed without granting any API access.

steps.title: Fix the one-page brief format
steps.body: Create a one-page brief document with fixed fields: offer, audience and ICP, intent clusters, competitive angle, and success metrics. Fill one out per campaign. A complete brief is the input contract — if a field is blank, the kit will be vague, so treat blanks as blockers.

steps.title: Set up the kit workbook
steps.body: Create a Google Sheets workbook with one tab each for ad group structure, copy variants, landing page outline, UTM builder, and QA checklist. Encode your naming conventions as data-validation rules or formulas so a wrongly named campaign shows up red before it ever reaches the account.

steps.title: Generate the kit with Claude
steps.body: Paste the brief and your naming conventions into Claude and ask for the ad group structure organized by theme, intent tier, and match type, plus three copy variants per ad group and a landing page outline mapped to each group's intent. Paste the output into the workbook tabs. Edit in the sheet, not in the chat — the workbook is the source of truth.

steps.title: Build UTMs in the sheet
steps.body: The UTM tab constructs final URLs by formula from your documented convention — source, medium, campaign, content, term. Nobody hand-types a UTM. Copy the finished URLs into the copy tab so every ad row carries its tracking.

steps.title: Run the QA checklist
steps.body: Work the checklist tab row by row: tracking in place, copy within character limits, targeting matches the brief, budget matches the plan, landing page matches the ad's promise. Any blocker stops the launch until it is resolved; warnings get a note for the reviewer.

steps.title: Upload paused via Google Ads Editor
steps.body: Import the structure and copy through Google Ads Editor with every campaign paused. A second person reviews in-account against the workbook, then enables. Because the upload is paused, backing out is as simple as deleting the draft — nothing has spent.

manualPath.cadence: Run once per campaign launch; with templates in place, a full cycle takes hours instead of the days a hand-built campaign takes.

mcpPath.overview: Your project management tool, ad account, and asset storage connect to an agent that runs the whole kit on a trigger: a launch-request task goes in, a paused and QA'd campaign comes out. The agent acts within hard caps and logs every step; you approve before anything spends. It suits teams launching often enough that copy-paste is the bottleneck.

steps.title: Connect the systems
steps.body: Give the agent your project management tool for launch tasks and approvals, the Google Ads API (or an MCP server for Google Ads) scoped to creating campaigns in a paused state, Google Sheets or Drive for the kit archive, and GA4 read-only for tracking checks. Wire the trigger with n8n, Zapier, Make, or Claude with MCP servers directly.

steps.title: Encode your launch process as the agent's instructions
steps.body: Write a persistent instruction file holding your offer, audience, naming conventions, budget caps, and required QA gates. This file is the governance layer: the agent refuses to build from an incomplete brief and cannot act outside what the file permits. It carries forward, so every future launch starts from the same guardrails.

steps.title: Agent assembles the kit
steps.body: When a launch task is tagged in your PM tool, the agent reads the brief fields and generates the strategic brief, ad group structure by theme, intent tier, and match type, three copy variants per group, landing page outlines, and UTMs built strictly from your taxonomy. Everything is written to the workbook archive before anything touches the ad account.

steps.title: Agent runs automated QA
steps.body: The agent validates every name against your convention, checks UTM parity between ads and landing pages, confirms budgets sit within cap, and verifies via GA4 that tracking is in place. Blockers halt the run; warnings are collected for the reviewer rather than silently cleared.

steps.title: Paused upload and approval gate
steps.body: The agent creates the campaigns through the ads API in a paused state with the capped daily budget already set, then posts the QA results and a summary of exactly what was built to the PM task. A named approver reviews and enables in-account. Nothing spends on the agent's authority alone.

steps.title: Log and improve
steps.body: Every run appends to the audit log: inputs, generated assets, QA outcomes, who approved, and when spend was enabled. After launch, feed what worked back into the instruction file so the next kit starts smarter without loosening any guardrail.

connections: Project management tool (Asana, Jira, or similar — API access or an MCP server) for launch requests and approvals
Google Ads API (or an MCP server for Google Ads), scoped to creating campaigns in a paused state
Google Sheets or Drive for the kit archive and audit log
GA4 (read-only) to verify tracking fires and UTM data lands correctly

tools: Claude with MCP servers — generates the brief, structure, and copy, and runs the QA loop on the connected path
Google Sheets — the kit workbook, formula-driven UTM builder, and QA checklist on both paths
Google Ads Editor — bulk paused uploads on the manual path, fully reversible before anything runs
Google Ads API — programmatic paused campaign creation on the MCP path
n8n, Zapier, or Make — wires the PM-tool launch trigger to the agent run
GA4 — confirms tracking fires and UTM data arrives clean
guardrails: Every campaign is created paused; only a named human approver enables spend
Daily budget caps are set at creation and the agent cannot exceed them — raising a cap is a human-only change
QA must pass with zero blockers before an approval request goes out; warnings are surfaced to the reviewer, never cleared silently
Every run writes an audit log — inputs, generated assets, QA results, approver, enable time — and a paused upload can be deleted with nothing spent
guides.metaDescription: Build the Campaign Launch Kit two ways: a manual spreadsheet workflow or an MCP-connected agent that assembles and QAs campaigns, paused until you approve.

guides.name: PPC Intelligence
guides.tagline: Classifies every search term by intent, drafts negatives, and shifts budget to what works.
guides.what:
PPC Intelligence watches what your ad spend actually buys. It classifies every search term that triggered your ads by intent, flags terms burning budget with zero conversions, builds negative keyword lists across match types, and drafts a budget reallocation plan that moves spend from underperforming campaigns to high-intent ones. A weekly digest tells you what changed, what it recommends next, and what needs your sign-off.

Compounding here looks like hygiene: the account gets cleaner every cycle. Negative lists keep bad traffic out permanently, the classification rules sharpen each time you correct an edge case, and the audit log becomes a memory of every decision — so each weekly pass starts from a better baseline than the last.
guides.outcome: Less budget leaks to zero-conversion search terms and more flows to high-intent campaigns — the sample sprint audit projects +34% conversion volume at the same monthly spend.
prereqs: A Google Ads account with clean conversion tracking — conversions fire reliably and map to real business outcomes
Google Ads API access for the MCP path, or export permissions for the manual path
Search terms report history — a few months is enough to see the waste patterns
GA4 landing page performance data; Search Console access is optional but recommended
A named budget owner who signs off on reallocations and sets the spend caps

manualPath.overview: You run the loop yourself on scheduled exports: pull the reports, classify terms in a spreadsheet, and apply every change by hand through Google Ads Editor. It suits a marketer managing one or a few accounts who wants the discipline of the system working before wiring up any APIs.

steps.title: Export your reports on a fixed day
steps.body: Each week, download the Google Ads campaign export (CSV), the search terms report, and GA4 landing page performance. Add Search Console data if you have it. Drop everything into a dated folder so each cycle is comparable to the last.

steps.title: Classify search terms in a spreadsheet
steps.body: Pivot the search terms report by spend and conversions. Tag each meaningful term as converting, informational, competitor, or irrelevant. You can paste batches into Claude to pre-classify, but you verify every tag before it drives a change.

steps.title: Build the negative keyword list
steps.body: Turn irrelevant and informational terms into exact and phrase negatives on a dedicated tab. Group them into shared campaign-level lists so one decision protects the whole account, not one ad group.

steps.title: Draft the budget reallocation plan
steps.body: Rank campaigns by cost per conversion and conversion volume. Propose shifting budget from the worst performers toward high-intent campaigns, and get sign-off from the budget owner before touching anything in the account.

steps.title: Apply changes with Google Ads Editor
steps.body: Upload the negative lists and budget edits in one batch. Record every change — date, entity, before and after value — in a change-log tab so any edit can be reversed later.

steps.title: Set guardrail rules in the platform
steps.body: Configure Google Ads automated rules as your safety net: pause a keyword when CPA exceeds your threshold for a set window, alert on sharp week-over-week CTR drops, and cap daily spend per campaign.

manualPath.cadence: Weekly for the export-classify-apply cycle (an hour or two once the workbook is built); monthly for the deeper budget reallocation pass.

mcpPath.overview: The same loop runs as a scheduled agent workflow: read-scoped connections pull the data, the agent classifies terms and drafts changes inside written guardrails, and only small, reversible actions ship without you. It suits teams with clean conversion tracking who are done copy-pasting CSVs every Monday.

steps.title: Schedule the read-scoped pulls
steps.body: Wire the agent — Claude with MCP servers, orchestrated by n8n or Make — to pull search terms, campaign performance, and GA4 landing page data on a weekly schedule. Reads and writes use separate credentials, so the write scope stays as narrow as possible.

steps.title: Write the guardrails file
steps.body: Codify the rules in a persistent AGENTS.md instruction file: the classification taxonomy, negative match-type policy, spend caps, CPA thresholds, and exactly which actions require human approval.

steps.title: Run the classification loop
steps.body: Each week the agent classifies new search terms, scores waste by spend against conversions, drafts negatives, and models budget shifts. It logs its reasoning against the taxonomy, so you can audit why any term got tagged the way it did.

steps.title: Act within caps, queue the rest
steps.body: Negative keyword additions under your agreed weekly limit are applied automatically into shared lists and logged. Budget changes, campaign pauses, and anything above threshold are queued as approval requests with the projected impact attached.

steps.title: Digest, approve, revert
steps.body: A weekly digest lands in Slack or email: what was applied, what awaits your approval, what was flagged. Every applied change carries before-and-after values in the audit log, so a bad call is one revert away.

connections: Google Ads (read-scoped API access or an MCP server for Google Ads; a separate, narrowly scoped write path for negatives and budgets)
GA4 (read-only API export or an MCP server for GA4)
Google Sheets or BigQuery — where the classification history and audit log live
Slack or email — where the weekly digest and approval requests land

tools: Google Ads Editor — bulk-applies negatives and budget edits on the manual path
Google Sheets — the classification workbook, reallocation model, and change log
Claude with MCP servers — classifies search terms by intent and drafts the digest and recommendations
n8n or Make — schedules the data pulls, runs the loop, and routes approval requests
BigQuery — optional history store when the account outgrows a spreadsheet
Google Ads automated rules — platform-native spend caps and anomaly alerts that run independently of the agent
guardrails: Negatives auto-apply only up to an agreed weekly count, and only into shared campaign-level lists; larger batches wait for approval
Every budget reallocation and campaign pause requires explicit human approval — the agent only auto-applies negatives
Hard daily spend caps run as native Google Ads automated rules, independent of the agent, so a failed workflow can never overspend
Every action is written to an audit log with before-and-after values, so any change can be reversed from the log
guides.metaDescription: PPC Intelligence build guide: run search term classification and budget reallocation via spreadsheet exports or an MCP-connected agent under spend caps.

guides.name: SEO/PPC Opportunity
guides.tagline: Finds queries you pay for but already rank for — and flags where budget should move.
guides.what:
On a set cadence, this automation joins your Google Ads search terms, Search Console queries, and GA4 landing page data, then sorts every query into three lists: cannibalization (you pay for clicks you already earn organically), content gaps (high-intent paid queries with no organic coverage), and budget reallocation candidates. Each run ends in an action pack — a negative keyword list, landing page briefs for the top gaps, and a proposed budget shift — not just a dashboard.

The reason to run it on a cadence is drift: paid and organic pull apart every month as new search terms appear, rankings move, and seasons change. A one-off overlap audit decays; a recurring one keeps catching the overlap as it re-forms, and the accumulated negative list and brief backlog become a channel split you actually govern.
guides.outcome: Cuts paid spend on queries where organic already wins — the clicks you are paying for but could earn for free — and redirects that budget to genuine content gaps.
prereqs: Standard or admin access to the Google Ads account, with Google Ads Editor installed for batch changes
A verified Search Console property with at least 3 months of query history
A GA4 property with conversion tracking on the landing pages that matter
An agreed cross-channel baseline: one shared answer to which conversions count as SEO vs. PPC

manualPath.overview: This version runs entirely on scheduled exports and one spreadsheet — no API work beyond your normal logins. It suits a marketer who owns both channels and can give the analysis part of a day each month. Every change is applied by hand, so the approval gate is you.

steps.title: Pull the three exports
steps.body: Export the Google Ads search terms report, the Search Console query report, and GA4 landing page performance, all for the same 3-month window. Drop each into its own tab of one workbook. If you track competitors, keep the domain list in a fourth tab for context.

steps.title: Normalize and join the queries
steps.body: Lowercase everything, strip match-type noise and near-duplicates, then join paid search terms against Search Console queries. For each matched query, line up paid clicks, cost, and conversions next to organic position, clicks, and CTR.

steps.title: Classify every query into three lists
steps.body: Cannibalization: strong organic position and you are still paying for the click. Gaps: paid queries that convert but have no organic coverage. Reallocation: spend concentrated where organic already carries the intent. Score each row by cost so the biggest overlaps rise to the top.

steps.title: Draft the action pack
steps.body: Turn the cannibalization list into a negative keyword list. Write short landing page briefs for the top gap queries — target query, intent, page angle. Add a one-paragraph budget note: what you would pause, what you would fund instead.

steps.title: Apply by hand with a change log
steps.body: Apply the negatives through Google Ads Editor as one reviewable batch. Log every change with the date and before/after values so you can reverse the whole batch if organic traffic does not pick up the slack. Next cycle, compare lists against this one — that trend becomes your channel attribution baseline.

manualPath.cadence: Monthly; a cycle takes roughly half a day once the workbook template exists.

mcpPath.overview: The same loop, run by an agent over live connections: read-scoped access to Google Ads, Search Console, and GA4, a scheduled job that joins and classifies queries, and an approval gate before anything touches the account. It suits teams with clean conversion tracking that want the cadence enforced by the system, not the calendar.

steps.title: Connect and reconcile the three sources
steps.body: Wire up Search Console, GA4, and Google Ads with read-only credentials. Before trusting the loop, run one pull and reconcile it against the reports you already use — if the numbers do not match your dashboards, fix that first.

steps.title: Write down the cross-channel baseline
steps.body: Agree which conversions belong to SEO and which to PPC, and encode that rule in the agent's instructions. Every recommendation gets scored against this shared model, so neither channel claims the other's wins.

steps.title: Schedule the gap-analysis run
steps.body: The orchestrator pulls the three datasets on your cadence, normalizes and joins the queries, and classifies each into cannibalization, gap, or reallocation. The agent drafts the negative keyword list, the gap briefs, and a proposed budget shift with the evidence rows attached.

steps.title: Hold recommendations for sign-off
steps.body: Each run posts a digest — to a Sheet, Slack, or email — listing proposed negatives, briefs, and budget moves with the data behind them. Nothing is applied until a human approves the batch. Rejected items are logged with a reason so the agent learns your thresholds.

steps.title: Apply within caps, log everything
steps.body: Approved negatives go live as one staged batch, via the API write path or exported to Google Ads Editor. Budget shifts above your per-cycle cap always escalate. Every applied change lands in an audit log with timestamp and before/after values, reversible as a batch.

steps.title: Review the loop monthly
steps.body: Sample the audit log, check the attribution baseline still holds, and tighten or loosen the classification thresholds. The overlap trend across runs is your evidence that the split between channels is actually improving.

connections: Google Ads (read-only API access or an MCP server; a scoped write path only for approved negative-keyword batches)
Google Search Console (API or an MCP server, read-only)
GA4 (Data API or a BigQuery export, read-only)
Google Sheets or Looker Studio (approval digest and audit log)
An orchestrator: n8n, Make, Zapier, or Claude with MCP servers

tools: Google Ads Editor — batch-apply approved negative keywords as one reviewable, reversible change set
Google Search Console — organic queries, positions, and CTR for the overlap join
GA4 — landing page and conversion data to weight each opportunity by value
Google Sheets — the overlap workbook, approval digest, and change log
Claude with MCP servers — runs the join, classify, and recommend loop on the integrated path
n8n, Zapier, or Make — schedules the pulls and routes the approval digest
guardrails: No auto-apply: negative keywords and budget moves ship only as an approved, staged batch — the agent recommends, a human releases
Set a per-cycle cap on budget reallocation; any proposed shift above it escalates for explicit sign-off
Every applied change is logged with timestamp and before/after values, and each batch is reversible in one step via Google Ads Editor or change history
Read-only scopes on Search Console and GA4 at all times; Google Ads write access limited to the negative-keyword path

guides.name: Event Follow-Up
guides.tagline: Attendee list to segmented, personalized first touch within 24 hours — not 3–5 days.
guides.what:
Event Follow-Up puts the attendee list from a trade show, webinar, or field event to work while the conversations are still warm. Badge scans or registration exports land in your CRM, get enriched with company, title, and LinkedIn data, are segmented by role, company size, and session attended — and each segment gets a personalized three-touch sequence, with the first touch out within 24 hours instead of the 3–5 days manual processing usually takes.

Reuse is the compounding here: the segmentation rules, sequence templates, and routing logic you build for one event carry straight to the next. Each run leaves cleaner CRM data and sharper segments, so every subsequent event starts closer to done.
guides.outcome: Speed to first touch: event leads receive a personalized first email within 24 hours instead of going cold over the 3–5 days manual processing takes.
prereqs: An attendee export you can get fast: badge scan file or event registration CSV
CRM access (HubSpot or Salesforce) with a field mapping you can write down
Email platform API access for loading sequences and enrolling contacts
A defined lead routing process — who counts as sales-ready, who goes to nurture
An ICP definition so segments and sequence copy have something to aim at

manualPath.overview: You work from the attendee export the event hands you — no live integrations required. Everything happens in a spreadsheet plus the CRM and email tools you already have, and you apply every change by hand. It suits teams that run a handful of events a year and want the discipline in place before the plumbing.

steps.title: Export and clean the attendee list
steps.body: The morning after the event closes, pull the badge scan file or registration CSV. Dedupe it against a fresh CRM contact export, normalize the columns you need — name, email, company, title, session attended — and drop obvious junk rows. This cleaned sheet is your working file for the whole cycle.

steps.title: Segment in the spreadsheet
steps.body: Add columns for role tier, company size, and session attended, then use filters and formulas to bucket every lead into a segment that maps to your ICP. Flag rows with missing company or title data and fill the gaps with a quick manual pass — the company website and LinkedIn cover most of them.

steps.title: Draft a three-touch sequence per segment
steps.body: For each segment, paste its profile — role, company size, what they attended — plus your ICP definition into Claude and draft a three-touch follow-up sequence. Edit until it sounds like you. Keep the approved copy in one doc so the next event starts from templates, not a blank page.

steps.title: Import leads and load sequences
steps.body: Import the segmented list into your CRM with an event tag and segment tags, then load each sequence into your email platform and enroll the matching segment. You press send — nothing goes out that you have not read.

steps.title: Route by hand and log it
steps.body: Apply your lead routing rules: sales-ready leads go to reps with a context note, nurture leads to the marketing list, unqualified contacts get suppressed. Record who went where in a routing tab of the working file so you have an audit trail and a baseline to tune against next event.

manualPath.cadence: Once per event. Start the morning after the event closes and first touches can go out the same day — inside the 24-hour window; budget roughly half a working day per event.

mcpPath.overview: Your CRM, email platform, and enrichment source are connected by API, and an agent runs the capture-to-route loop continuously: leads arrive from a badge scan or form fill and come out enriched, segmented, and enrolled, with a human approving copy and anything above threshold. It suits teams running frequent events that need the 24-hour first touch without a person handling every row.

steps.title: Wire the capture trigger
steps.body: Set up a webhook from your badge scan app or registration form through n8n, Zapier, or Make that pushes each new lead into the CRM within minutes of collection. Every record is tagged with an event ID and a run ID, so the agent knows exactly which contacts belong to this run.

steps.title: Enrich and segment automatically
steps.body: The agent picks up each new record, calls a data enrichment API to append company, title, and LinkedIn data, then applies your segmentation rules — role, company size, session attended. Segment tags are written back to the CRM, and every write is logged against the run ID.

steps.title: Generate copy, gate the send
steps.body: The agent drafts a three-touch sequence per segment from your ICP definition and loads it into the email platform as drafts. A human reviews and approves each segment's copy once per event before anything sends. This gate is not optional — the agent enrolls contacts, but you release the sequences.

steps.title: Route within caps
steps.body: Routing rules run automatically: sales-ready leads land in rep queues with context, nurture leads join the marketing list, unqualified contacts are suppressed. A daily cap limits how many leads can be auto-marked sales-ready; anything over the cap or ambiguous goes to a holding queue for human triage.

steps.title: Audit and roll back
steps.body: Every enrichment write, enrollment, and routing decision lands in an audit log keyed to the run ID, so a bad run can be reversed by tag in one pass. Review the log after each event, check where the agent hesitated or misrouted, and tighten the rules before the next one.

connections: CRM (an MCP server for HubSpot or Salesforce, or the native API) — read contacts, write only scoped fields and tags
Email platform API — create sequence drafts and enroll contacts, sends gated on approval
A data enrichment API — append company, title, and LinkedIn data to new leads
n8n, Zapier, or Make — carries the badge-scan or registration webhook into the CRM

tools: Google Sheets — the manual path's working surface for dedupe, segmentation, and the routing log
HubSpot or Salesforce APIs — where leads live; both paths read from and write back to the CRM
n8n, Zapier, or Make — moves the badge-scan or form webhook into the CRM in near real time
Claude with MCP servers — drafts per-segment sequence copy and, on the MCP path, runs the enrich-segment-route loop
Your email platform's API — loads sequences and enrolls segments, with sends held for approval
guardrails: No email sends without a human approving each segment's sequence copy — the agent drafts, you release
Daily cap on leads auto-routed as sales-ready; anything over the cap queues for human review
Every CRM write is tagged with a run ID and logged, so a bad run reverses by tag in one pass
Agent scope is limited to contacts on the event list — it cannot touch or email the rest of your database
guides.metaDescription: Event Follow-Up: get event leads a first touch in 24 hours. Build it manually with exports and sheets, or as a governed agent via MCP connections.

guides.name: Lead Reactivation
guides.tagline: Scores dormant CRM leads, personalizes outreach, and routes engaged replies to sales.
guides.what:
Lead Reactivation works the pipeline you already paid for. It pulls every CRM contact with 90-plus days of no activity, scores each one on recency, source, and ICP fit, and sorts them into three tracks: high-ICP re-engagement, mid-fit value reintroduction, and long-dormant win-back-or-suppress. Each track gets its own 4–6 touch sequence, with industry, role, and original lead source shaping the subject line, pain hook, and call to action.

Because the loop feeds itself, the returns stack. Engaged leads route straight to sales, non-responders move to nurture or a suppression list, and every send, open, and routing decision lands in a trigger/action/impact log. Each cycle leaves your list cleaner and your scoring sharper — which matters, because most companies are sitting on 12–18 months of leads nobody ever properly followed up on.
guides.outcome: Turns contacts dormant 90+ days into active sales conversations, recovering the 12–18 months of unworked leads most CRMs are holding.
prereqs: CRM read/write access (HubSpot, Salesforce, or similar) with permission to add tags and update lead status
Structured lead data on most records: last activity date, lead source, and basic firmographics
A written ICP definition — the firmographic and behavioural signals you can score against
An email platform that supports segmented sequences and suppression lists
Historical email performance (opens, clicks, bounces) for a deliverability baseline

manualPath.overview: Run the whole loop on a monthly CRM export and a scoring spreadsheet — no integrations required. You do the scoring in Google Sheets, load sequences into your email platform by hand, and route engaged leads yourself. It suits teams with one CRM, a few thousand dormant leads, and no engineering support.

steps.title: Export your dormant leads
steps.body: In your CRM, filter for contacts with no activity in 90+ days and export to CSV. Include last activity date, lead source, campaign attribution, firmographics, and email engagement history. This file is the raw input for everything that follows.

steps.title: Score and segment in a spreadsheet
steps.body: Build scoring columns for recency band, source quality, and ICP fit (industry and role match against your written ICP). Bucket leads into three segments: high-ICP and recently dormant, mid-fit, and long-dormant or low-fit. Before anything else, flag hard bounces and prior unsubscribes into a suppression tab so they never enter a sequence.

steps.title: Draft three sequences, review every message
steps.body: Write a 4–6 touch sequence per segment. Use Claude to draft variants keyed to segment attributes — an industry pain hook, a role-specific CTA, a subject line that references the original source. You read and approve every message before anything ships; nothing goes out unreviewed.

steps.title: Load segments and send in capped batches
steps.body: Import each segment into your email platform as a static list and schedule the sequence. Cap daily sends to protect deliverability rather than blasting the whole list at once. Tag every imported lead in the CRM so you can identify — and undo — everything this workflow touched.

steps.title: Review engagement weekly and route by hand
steps.body: Export opens, clicks, and replies back into the sheet. Anyone who clicked gets a CRM task assigned to sales. Opened-but-no-click after three sends moves to your nurture list. No opens after the full sequence gets a suppressed tag for list hygiene.

steps.title: Keep a trigger/action/impact log
steps.body: Maintain a running tab: date, segment, action taken, result. That log is your audit trail, your report to leadership, and the input that improves next month's scoring weights.

manualPath.cadence: Monthly export-and-score cycle; once sequences are live, the weekly engagement review takes about an hour.

mcpPath.overview: Connect your CRM and email platform to an agent via MCP so the loop runs continuously instead of monthly. The agent scores newly dormant leads, drafts personalized touches from an approved rules engine, and routes engaged replies — while you approve copy and anything above the caps. It suits teams with API access to their stack and a steady flow of leads going cold.

steps.title: Connect read-scoped first, grant narrow writes later
steps.body: Start with read-only connections to your CRM and email platform and let the agent score leads without touching anything. Once you've validated its scoring against a sample you check by hand, grant write scope limited to three things: tags, sequence enrollment, and suppression status. Nothing broader.

steps.title: Encode the scoring and personalization rules
steps.body: The segmentation model and personalization rules live in a versioned config the agent reads — recency bands, ICP signals, and the mappings from segment attributes to message variables. The agent can propose rule changes with evidence from the log, but it cannot change its own rules silently.

steps.title: Run the loop on a schedule
steps.body: On a daily or weekly schedule (n8n, Zapier, or Make works as the scheduler), the agent pulls contacts that just crossed the 90-day dormancy line, scores them, assigns a segment, drafts touches from your approved templates, and enrolls them — always inside the daily send cap.

steps.title: Gate what matters, auto-approve what's safe
steps.body: New copy variants, any enrollment batch above your threshold, and any change to routing rules land in Slack or email for your sign-off before execution. Conservative actions — suppressing bounced addresses, tagging for list hygiene — are pre-approved because they only ever shrink risk.

steps.title: Route and log automatically
steps.body: A click routes the lead to the sales queue as a high-priority CRM task. Opens with no click after three sends move the lead to nurture. Every trigger, action, and outcome writes to the impact log with a timestamp and the specific rule that fired.

steps.title: Review weekly, tighten, and stay reversible
steps.body: You review the impact log, spot-check routed leads, and adjust scoring weights or caps. Because every write the agent makes carries an automation tag, any batch of enrollments or routings can be isolated with one filter and rolled back.

connections: CRM (an MCP server for HubSpot or Salesforce, or their APIs) — read contacts and activity; write tags, enrollment, and routing status
Email platform API — enroll leads in sequences; read opens, clicks, replies, and bounces
Google Sheets or BigQuery — the trigger/action/impact log and scoring history
n8n, Zapier, or Make — schedules the loop and delivers approval requests
Slack or email — the human approval channel

tools: HubSpot or Salesforce APIs — the system of record for leads, tags, and routing status
Google Sheets — the scoring model and trigger/action/impact log on the manual path
Claude with MCP servers — drafts segment-personalized copy and runs the scoring loop on the agent path
n8n, Zapier, or Make — schedules the loop and carries approval requests to Slack or email
BigQuery — engagement history at a scale a spreadsheet can't hold
Looker Studio — a simple dashboard over the impact log for the weekly review
guardrails: Approval gate: new sequence copy and any enrollment batch larger than the threshold you set (a few hundred leads is a sane default) require human sign-off before sending
Send caps: a daily send ceiling protects deliverability — the agent queues sends, it never bursts the full list
Reversibility: every CRM write carries an automation tag, so any batch of tags, enrollments, or routings can be filtered out and rolled back in one pass
Audit log: every trigger, action, and routing outcome is recorded with a timestamp and the rule that fired — nothing moves without a record
guides.metaDescription: Build a Lead Reactivation automation two ways: a manual CRM-export and spreadsheet workflow, or an MCP-connected agent with approval gates and send caps.

hub.intro:
Every guide in this library assumes the same foundation: access to your data, a place to work on it, and rules decided before anything runs. Set it up once and every build starts at the interesting part instead of a week of access requests — with governance built into the environment, not bolted onto each workflow afterwards.

Each guide ships two build paths. The manual path runs on exports and a spreadsheet — you are the automation, and it genuinely works. The MCP path connects Claude to your stack through read-scoped connections that act within caps you set, log everything, and wait for your approval. The layers below serve both.

sections.title: Data access & exports
sections.body: Everything downstream depends on clean, repeatable access to your ad platforms, analytics, and CRM. Start read-only everywhere: you can diagnose, plan, and build an entire workflow before anything needs permission to change an account. Write access comes later, per workflow, with caps attached.

items.name: Google Ads
items.desc: Install Google Ads Editor and download your campaigns for offline review, or pull search terms and performance reports from the web interface. The step that matters: create a dedicated read-only user on the account instead of sharing an admin login — you can widen its access later, and every guide here starts from what that user can see.

items.name: Meta Ads
items.desc: Grant analyst-level (read) access through Business settings rather than adding another admin, then export campaign and ad-set reports from Ads Manager. Confirm the export includes the conversion columns you actually optimize against before you build anything on top of it.

items.name: GA4
items.desc: Grant Viewer access on the property and export the reports your workflows need — landing pages, conversions, traffic sources. Before you trust any export, verify your key conversion events are firing; an automation built on a broken event fires confidently in the wrong direction.

items.name: CRM (HubSpot, Salesforce)
items.desc: Set up a read-only user or profile and export the lists and reports your workflows draw on — lifecycle stage, source, last activity. Agree on which fields are canonical first, so every workflow reads the same definition of a lead.

items.name: API credentials (when a guide calls for them)
items.desc: Some builds use the Google or Meta ads APIs directly. Request read scopes first, store the credentials in your integration platform's credential store — never in a spreadsheet or a chat thread — and record who holds them.

sections.title: The spreadsheet workspace
sections.body: The manual path of every guide runs in a spreadsheet, and a well-structured one is a legitimate automation environment — it just runs on your schedule instead of a trigger. Set the structure once and every export drops into the same shape.

items.name: One Google Sheet per workflow
items.desc: Resist the mega-spreadsheet. One file per workflow keeps each build portable, shareable, and safe to hand to a teammate. Name files by workflow and date range, not "final-final".

items.name: A standard tab structure
items.desc: Raw, Working, Output, Log — in that order. Paste exports only into Raw tabs and build formulas only in Working tabs, so a fresh import never overwrites a month of logic.

items.name: A change-log tab
items.desc: Date, what changed, who approved it. This is the manual version of the audit log the agent path keeps automatically, and it is the habit that makes the upgrade painless — you already know what a good log looks like.

items.name: Looker Studio for the shared view
items.desc: When someone else needs to see results, connect Looker Studio to the Output tab only. Stakeholders get a live dashboard; your working formulas stay untouched.

sections.title: Integration platforms
sections.body: When a workflow proves itself manually, an integration platform runs it on a schedule. You need one, not all three — pick based on how much control you want over complexity and where your data lives, and set up alerting before the first run, not after the first silent failure.

items.name: Zapier
items.desc: The fastest route for simple, linear connections — a new lead lands, a row gets added, a notification fires. Set it up under a team account with shared ownership, not someone's personal login that leaves when they do.

items.name: Make
items.desc: Visual scenarios with real branching and error routes, a good fit once workflows have conditions and multiple outcomes. Turn on error notifications for every scenario before you activate it — a workflow that fails quietly is worse than no workflow.

items.name: n8n
items.desc: Self-hostable, which means full control over where your data flows and sits — often the deciding factor for agencies handling client data or teams with data-residency requirements. Decide who hosts it and who can edit workflows before you build the first one.

items.name: Shared credentials, wherever you land
items.desc: Store every platform connection in the tool's credential store under the read-only users you created in the first layer. When a workflow later needs write access, that becomes a deliberate, visible upgrade — not a default.

sections.title: A lightweight warehouse
sections.body: You do not need a data warehouse to start — a well-kept Sheet is one. But there is a predictable moment when you outgrow it, and knowing the growth path in advance means you never rebuild from scratch.

items.name: Google Sheets as warehouse zero
items.desc: For a single workflow reading a weekly export, a Raw tab is your warehouse. It stops being enough when multiple workflows need the same data, or the export outgrows what a spreadsheet handles comfortably.

items.name: BigQuery as the growth path
items.desc: GA4 links natively to BigQuery, landing raw event data you can query directly. Set up the link early even if you will not query it for months — the export begins the day you connect it, and you cannot backfill what was never collected.

items.name: Scheduled loads for everything else
items.desc: Ad platform and CRM exports can flow into BigQuery on a schedule through your integration platform or available connectors. One load, many workflows reading from it — instead of the same export pasted into three sheets.

items.name: The graduation signal
items.desc: When you catch yourself pasting the same export into more than one spreadsheet, or manually joining two exports every week, that is the signal. Move that dataset to BigQuery and point the sheets at it.

sections.title: The agent layer
sections.body: This is where the MCP path in each guide runs: Claude connected to your stack, reading real data and either recommending actions or taking them inside limits you define. It is autonomous within bounds, not a black box — the governance pieces here are not optional extras, they are the setup.

items.name: Claude with MCP servers
items.desc: Connect Claude to each data source through an MCP server — one per ad platform, one for analytics, one for your CRM. Start every connection read-scoped: the agent can read live data, diagnose problems, and draft recommendations without the ability to change anything.

items.name: An AGENTS.md instruction file
items.desc: A plain-text file that defines what the agent may do, what it must never touch, and where its thresholds sit. Write it before you grant any write scope, and keep it in version control so changes to the rules are as visible as changes to the accounts they govern.

items.name: Approval gates
items.desc: Decide up front which actions execute automatically and which queue for a human — by dollar amount, by percentage change, by action type. A sensible starting posture: the agent recommends everything and executes nothing until you have watched its judgment for a few cycles.

items.name: Spend caps and scope limits
items.desc: When you do grant write access, attach hard limits at the same moment: a daily spend cap, a maximum change per run, a bounded set of accounts or campaigns it may touch. Caps live in the environment, so no single workflow can quietly exceed them.

items.name: The action log
items.desc: Every recommendation and every action gets recorded — what triggered it, what was done, what changed as a result. Put the log somewhere the whole team can read without asking. If an action is not in the log, it did not happen inside the system.

sections.title: Scheduling & monitoring
sections.body: A workflow you cannot see and cannot stop is not automated — it is unattended. The last layer makes every workflow observable and reversible, which is what lets you extend its autonomy with confidence rather than hope.

items.name: Cadence that matches the decision
items.desc: Schedule each workflow to the rhythm of the decision it supports, not "as often as possible." Faster than the decision cycle just generates noise nobody reads; each guide names its own cadence.

items.name: Alerts on failure and on silence
items.desc: Route errors and threshold breaches to a channel someone actually watches. Then alert on silence too: a workflow that stops running produces no errors, and its absence is the failure you will notice last.

items.name: A kill-switch you have tested
items.desc: One documented step that stops everything — deactivate the scenario, revoke the credential, pause the agent. Test it before go-live and make sure more than one person can pull it. Reversibility is what makes bounded autonomy safe to extend.

items.name: A standing log review
items.desc: Put fifteen minutes on the calendar each week to read the action log: what ran, what it changed, what queued for approval. This is how thresholds earn loosening — the agent gets more room because the log shows it deserves it.

hub.metaDescription: Set up the shared environment behind every build guide: read-only data access, a spreadsheet workspace, integration platforms, and a governed agent layer.
